You're not giving me a first kiss. You're not holding my hand. It's not going anywhere. And I just time out. So this is how human emotions work. Let me take one more question here that I thought was good. Orrin, great meeting you at the event last weekend. Can you clarify how the verbal pitch flows with the pitch decks? For me, the pitch deck, again, is the landing page to get people to the phone call, to the meeting, to the interaction, and it is supportive of it. So during the interaction, they'll have the pitch deck to access. But then the meeting is to take them to a new level. They've already seen the pitch deck and it's good as a point of reference. But the meeting or the phone call or the Skype or the webinar should rise, should be leverage off of the pitch deck, but rise above it and continue the themes. But it should be novel and it should be intriguing. Intriguing, right? And now you'll actually give your pitch. So I'll summarize it like this. The pitch deck is not the pitch. It is supportive of it. It introduces the themes and some intrigue. But your pitch should not be direct parallel of the pitch deck. It should build on it and rise to new levels of interest, intrigue, insight, interpretation, investigation, like five I words. As a real author, I would write a book called The Five Eyes. I'm not. Okay. So that's my view of how the pitch deck and the pitch work with each other. And lots of people ask me, hey, did you use a PowerPoint or something on screen? I almost never do because I believe at least when you're meeting in person, I mean, look at this webinar, you know, we have one spied up through the whole thing. If you're strong enough and you have enough charisma and presentation capability and your project is deep enough and your ideas are rich and novel and intriguing enough, and you can deliver it in sufficient social context in a short enough period of time with appropriate comic timing and tonality. And you yourself are a compelling character that's worth paying attention to. You don't need a fucking PowerPoint to distract people because this is your chance to connect with humans. And you point them up at the screen away from you and they disconnect. So to degree that you feel comfortable controlling a room and delivering insight and providing novelty and sharing color and something fascinating and new ideas that rise to new levels that haven't been, you know, come seen before by the audience. And you yourself have depth in that field. You shouldn't need a PowerPoint. The PowerPoint will only reduce. The PowerPoint is a crutch. I'm not saying don't use it. Every time you feel yourself getting stronger socially able to bring more novelty and entry and new things and every time you feel like you have more color to add to the conversation, reduce the size of the PowerPoint. So if it starts out of 15 pages, you've got a fucking 15 page crutch on your hand. And I'm not saying we don't need crutches. I need it starting now depending on what level it is. Depending on what level you're at. But the PowerPoint is a crutch. So every time you feel you can add more value, take a page out until you have a one page PowerPoint and you are the focal point of the attention in the room because you have frame control. So I'd like to, you know, none of this is, hey, listen to Pitch Mastery and then the next day, you know, be the greatest in the world. But you'll be on your way, that's for sure. So you should be able to reduce the size of your PowerPoint every time you get better at this. I mean, I would say if you're, so if you're pitching a deal, not so much a product, but if you're pitching a deal and your PowerPoint is 16 pages, here's a way to measure it. Every meeting that you have with a real kind of potential joint venture partner or investor or business development partner or whatever, every meeting you should be getting that much better. If you're pushing yourself hard, you'll be able to take one slide out. So if you're starting off with 16 slides after 15 meetings, you should be down to one slide. I believe that. If it's not working out for you, email us to figure out something going, the train is off the tracks a little bit. This makes it easier and it raises your pitch to close ratio and it lets you have fun while you're doing it. That's the purpose. But don't, you know, don't not give your pitch because you don't feel like it's perfectly aligned and strong. But I mean, to that point, shorter, more concise, get, make sure you have pricing and frame control done. Give your actual pitch as quickly as you feel like you are strong and then grow your program over time. That's my, Jackson, that's what I think is right for you. Orrin, thank you. We've got, Jackson says, she will find a comedian I like. Okay. Orrin, so we'll get a couple more questions here. This is from Allison. It's a two-part question. Just touching on the idea of removing your PowerPoint, she says, how about when you're pitching media content where images are key? That seems fairly simple. There is no, so I guess I'm not fully, fully sure about all the context on this. Okay. But 20 minutes for everything. I mean, just the limits of human attention don't expand beyond 20 minutes on a pitch. Now, you can reboot, bounce them out of the room, do a location shift. We've talked about, do, you know, develop some new intrigue, get some kind of mental reboot going and go for another 20 minutes. All right. But even this agency that I met with, even after introducing them to strong this last, this week, shit, sometimes we drone on for 45 minutes on a pitch, we can see immediately now, that's too long, you know, and the head of their program, you know, says, hey, we have got to shorten these, we've got to shorten these pitches down to 20 minutes because we can see exactly what the, you know, why. So it's got to hold it down to 20 minutes, whether it's one or all of them. So here's the thing, I have rich media that I have to show as well. All right. But what I do is I build as much tension and kind of seriousness as possible until it's almost like too much and then okay, we're going to watch some media now. And I use it as a reboot or a reliever, but I don't have the media up all the time. I'm not showing the media constantly. I say you have to show rich media, but you should talk about it in terms of the big idea, the economics, the ROI. Okay. I mean, people care about deals, not about the actual media. That's just the product, you know, they need a deal, they need to make money with it. They need to make money with you. They need to buy it and then go do something with it, add some value and make money. That's the deal. Spend most of the time talking about the deal and then show the rich media within context of it. But I think you're constantly going to be fighting for attention if you're showing rich media in the same time that you're pitching your deal. So David, it's a good one to follow up on because I'm not familiar with everybody's circumstances and there's exceptions to every rule, but this is what it sounds to me like. Well, thank you for your time and the opportunity to share with you my idea for investing your money for retirement. Investors today are frustrated. They're angry and overwhelmed in the aftermath of the collapse of the capital markets in recent years. You, like most investors, are probably fed up with Wall Street and searching for answers. Not long ago, we had the Enron fraud followed by Bernie Madoff and then MF Global, and then now JPMorgan with billions of dollars of unaccounted losses. Interest rates aren't historically as the Fed attempts to prop up the fragile economy. The 10-year Treasury is under 2% and banks offer CD rates less than 1%. Now we've got the Greek drama threatening to unravel the Euro, which presents untold risk and uncertainty to the global markets. So what's an investor to do? How do you build wealth in the tumult and risk and uncertainty of the markets in this great recession we've got? In 08, the highly acclaimed and illustrious hedge fund manager, Jim Cramer, proclaimed that Bear Stearns was a solid investment just days before it collapsed. Here's our challenge. We all want financial freedom, right? How do we buy our freedom back? Because essentially, that's what we're trying to do. Meanwhile, Wall Street has brainwashed us to be gamblers. Does Jim Cramer and all those talking heads on CNBC and on the financial networks, do they really have all the answers? And are we really going to base our investment decisions on a two-second soundbite of buy, sell, or hold? We're trained to believe that somehow we can figure out how to out-trade the insiders, the hedge funds, the supercomputers. You may have even seen the ING commercial. What's your number? The premise of the ad operates under flawed logic. How are you supposed to know what the value of the market is going to be long-term? How are you supposed to know what the future tax rates are going to be? Cost of living. Do you know when you're going to die? So how are we going to invest so that we can eventually stop working? You can only stop working when you've got enough passive income to replace your regular working income. But even a whole pile of money doesn't really work. Look at a big pile of money. Look at Mike Tyson. He had 400 million. He blew through that. Poor decisions and poor investments wiped him out. An analogy I like to use is like a flight from, let's say, LA to New York. The plane ascends to 30,000 feet, peak altitude, right? So, Wall Street wants you to amass this pile of capital and at 65 retirement age, you turn off the engine and consume the principal. Well, in my view, that's a real risky way to go, but that's what most people do. This is insanity. I present an alternative to the speculation and the insanity of Wall Street. Invest for income. Our problem is Wall Street is fraught with danger and risk. We've got rampant insider trading, high frequency trading, which comprises anywhere from half to 70% of all trades. High volatility. There have been a couple times in US history when the market was flat for many years. Do you want to bend in commodities? Who knows what's going to happen to gold? It goes up, it goes down. No commodity goes up forever. And finally, you can't eat capital gain. Only 4 to 5% of Americans at age 65 can ever retire with any kind of financial security. So, most people are clueless. The solution lies in following an investment strategy that relies on income generation, not speculation. The solution I present to you is mobile home parks. Why, you ask? They generate cash flow now without speculation. They are recession proof, inflation proof, and even technology proof. Humans will always need a place to live. Apple won't be able to figure out some substitute for that. Warren Buffett likes the mobile home space. He's invested about $7 billion in mobile homes and mobile home parks. He bought Clayton Homes in 2003 for $1.7 billion. And his company is the largest holder of... I'm going to jump in at this point. I'm going to jump in. How do you get in the channel? Where is this message mainly delivered? Is this in a deck? It's on an internet site. Well, eventually it will be on the internet. I'm writing a book about it. Status comes from three things. All right. Not my opinion, but cognitive psychologists who understand what people respond to as high status. Okay. It comes from money. So, 100 millionaires and billionaires and having money is a source of status. Having power, the mayor of New York, the secretary of defense, being able to march all human resources and make decisions that affect many, many people, being a senator has status. And being a celebrity. So, celebrity power and money are the three primary ways of conveying status. What you need to do in this business is come from a position of high status. The way we talk about in the book and within pitch mastery is to create local star power. That's what the cognitive guys call it. This is not some kind of fancy language I came up with, local star power. And the way you do that is providing some insight that is relevant, deep, moves people forward and sets you up as a source of valuable information. So, my issue is, you know, made off grease, low treasuries is broad to... And this is what I think is the core messaging that could be massively improved. Is that is that's broad messaging that everybody you can build rapport with it because people go, yeah, I'm familiar with those problems. But it doesn't give you any status. Like they know about that stuff. You're just combining it into one aggregate pool. So, you need some more focus. The way I do with my fund, right, because they sell a fixed income product. So, say, hey, look, if you're looking for 9 to 10 percent yields in the market, okay, which is a good, you know, risk reward focus, that's hard to do. And so, you've got to be looking at distress notes, hedge fund investments, tertiary market real estate, and factoring. Because that's where you can get that kind of yield. In the same light, you should be considering CNI loans, commercial and industrial loans, in the lower middle market, which can achieve the same yields with a lower risk profile. This deck is dedicated to making money with a risk adjusted return of 9 to 11 percent in by making CNI loans in the lower middle market. Okay, so they have the exact same issues that you've talked about. Hey, it's hard to make money, treasuries are low, you know, shit is blowing up all over the place. What do you do with your money?
